frameworks.name: Replicate Before Iterate
frameworks.definition: A two-phase learning discipline: in phase one, follow instructions exactly and replicate the teacher's results without modification; in phase two, only after replication is confirmed, apply accumulated knowledge to diverge and derive personal improvements.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that the correct sequence for closing knowledge gaps is to replicate before iterating: follow someone's system to the letter until you have reproduced their results, and only then apply your own knowledge to modify or improve it. Iterating before replication produces changes that are not grounded in an understanding of what actually drove the original results.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: learning a new skill or system from an expert or teacher
claims.mechanism: Replicating the original results first proves that the learner has internalized the system correctly; only then can changes be attributed to meaningful improvement rather than random deviation.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that paying for access to information and operators is faster than self-directed free learning, because the purchase compresses time-to-competence rather than guaranteeing a specific outcome. The value of paid education is time arbitrage, not certainty.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: operator seeking to close a specific knowledge gap
paid resource exists covering the target domain
claims.mechanism: Purchased access concentrates prior practitioners' experience, reducing the learner's trial-and-error cycle; the cost buys down time, not risk elimination.
bounded_by: Paid education does not guarantee the outcome — the speaker explicitly states that no single purchase solves everything.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that he has been so consistently rewarded from investing in his own education that he structures his life by default to maximize ongoing learning exposure, treating education spend as a recurring capital allocation rather than a one-time decision.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi and Leila Hormozi apply a shared-knowledge-exposure rule as business partners: they never join a mastermind separately, never attend events separately, and when one reads a book the other receives the core summary — ensuring both partners always share the same information base and informational stimuli.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: expose each other to the same information, the same stimulus
conditions: founder-operator partnerships where both partners are involved in strategy

claims.text: Alex Hormozi and Leila Hormozi declined to join a premium network that required one of them to lie or to join separately rather than as a pair, choosing to forgo the network access rather than violate their integrity standard. The speaker frames integrity as a non-negotiable that supersedes high-value network opportunities.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: I will not forgo my integrity

examples.text: Alex Hormozi recounts that by always aiming to be the best student and best success story of every program he purchased, he consistently over-delivered — which then caused each program vendor to publicly claim credit for his outcomes. He describes this as having backfired because roughly ten different people have at various times publicly claimed they were the catalyst behind his launch success, even though the results were driven by his own execution.

Alex Hormozi and Leila Hormozi were accepted to a super-premium network that required only one member per partnership. The network operators first said no to both joining, then privately asked Leila (or Alex) to lie about the arrangement so both could get in while circumventing the rules. The Hormozis declined: they refused to lie and required a package deal or nothing, ultimately walking away from the network entirely to preserve their integrity standard.

practices.text: Spend money wherever you can to learn more, and set up your life so that by default you are continuously increasing your knowledge exposure.

When in a business partnership, read the same books, attend the same events, and share the cliff notes whenever one partner consumes content the other has not yet seen — so neither partner operates from an information asymmetry.
